title: Health-First Systems

content.question: What's your pre-stress regulation habit, or do you only cope after?
sop: Build one daily bottom-up regulation habit, breathwork, cold exposure, movement, before you open email or start work
Do a 60-second body check before any high-stakes task: breath rate, jaw tension, shoulders
Rate your nervous system state 1-10 before starting your top priority for the day (see calculation, page 6)
Use a quick reset (a few slow breaths, a short walk) the moment you notice a stress spike
Track which regulation habit actually moves the needle for you, and drop the ones that don't
content.explainer: Your capacity to think clearly, decide well, and execute isn't separate from your physical state, it runs through it. A dysregulated nervous system doesn't just feel bad, it measurably narrows attention and worsens judgment, which is why the highest-leverage move before a hard task is often not more strategy but ninety seconds of regulation first.
questions: What's your pre-stress regulation habit, or do you only cope after?
How much of your work output today happened while your body was actually in a stressed state, and did you notice while it was happening?
What's the first physical sign your nervous system gives you before a bad decision, and do you act on it or override it?
content.equation_example: Rate your regulation 1-10 before a task. At a 3 (dysregulated), decision quality and output noticeably drop. At an 8 (regulated), the same task gets done faster with fewer errors. Higher balance means clearer function; lower balance means your capacity is there but hard to access.
content.closing_statement: A calm nervous system outperforms a motivated one.

caption:
Principle Nº 216: Health-First Systems

Regulate nervous system before tasks. Your capacity to think clearly, decide well, and execute isn't separate from your physical state, it runs through it.

Ask yourself: What's your pre-stress regulation habit, or do you only cope after?
